speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTS] Her name is [? Aaron ?] Stern. She was engaged to my brother. Next to [POINTS] her is my sister, Frieda. And I'm right behind her. [POINTS] You could only see my head, my blond hair. Next to her [POINTS] is [? Jannika. ?] That's her aunt in the white kerchief. The tall woman, [POINTS] her name is [? Kasey ?] Kraus. She's my very good friend. That tall woman is my Aunt Rose. Second from the right is my father. His name is Benny Egri. He was in Auschwitz-Birkenau. And he lived there till December, and then he was killed. And next to him, where you could only see the little head, a little boy, he's my cousin. His name is Wally Hoffman. We don't know what happened to him. He probably got killed over there. The tall boy who wears the star, he's my cousin. And he's the son of Rozsika, who is my aunt, who was my brother's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture was taken at Auschwitz when they were already separated from the other group. So actually, my father was-- was separated to work, because he did live there for a half a year in Auschwitz-Birkenau. This is, from the second from the right, is my father. His name is Benny Egri. And this was taken in the Auschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp when we arrived in 1944 of May. And the little cousin, who you could only see his head, his name is Wally Hoffman. And he came in with us at the same time. That tall boy with the Jewish star on him, he's my cousin. He's the son of my Aunt Rose. I can't recall his name, but we came in together to the concentration camp. This is my mother from the left, who's holding my little cousin. My mother's name is Lena Egri. My little cousin's name is Judy Hoffman. The little boy over there is Herschel Hoffman. And the little girl, you could only see the head, her name is Ibolya Hoffman. Then the fourth woman, adult, is my mother's sister. Her name is [? Maulvin ?] Hoffman. And she must hold the other little girl's hand where you can't see it. And they are standing right in front of the crematorium. This is when we were separated from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, in May. This picture was taken in May, 1944, by an SS man when the Hungarian Jews came in. And in the printed dress, next to her, is my sister in the very first row. Behind her, you could only see my head, because she was always protecting me, so nobody would take her-- me away from her. We were standing here already after our hair was cut off with the many other prisoners, where we were unrecognizable.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in May of 1944 in Auschwitz-Birkenau. Yeah. This picture comes from the Auschwitz album. When we arrived in Auschwitz, and the selection started, the people, as you see far back, already is going towards the crematorium. In one hour, what you see here, the people, they were all killed. Their crematorium were very, very organized, so they could kill people as fast as they were coming in. And this was taken in May, 1944, in Auschwitz-Birkenau. Yeah. This is my brother, Meir Egri. He went home after the liberation to Hungary, our little town, and then they escaped to Italy. And he's there with his little girl. Her name is Leah, named after my mother. And-- Yes. This place was a beautiful place in Sweden. The name was [INAUDIBLE]. That was near Stockholm. The Swedish government let us use that for schooling, for girls' schooling, of the survivors. And we stayed here about a half a year in this place. I am in-- this picture was taken in [INAUDIBLE] in Sweden. And I'm the sixth, top row, in this picture. This was a girls' school for us through the Swedish government. And, in this picture, in Sweden, I went to visit my sister. And I'm the first one from the right, standing next to my sister. And I have many friends there in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the second row. This picture was taken in Ronninge in 1945-- '46 This was our home, three villa, three houses. It was a very beautiful place. And the girls, we stayed there at a girls' school. This is where we finished our year. This is my sister, Frieda, and myself. This was taken in Stockholm in Sweden. The year, maybe it was in 1947 or '48. Since then I lost her, and I miss her very, very much because she really saved my life. And I hope God will look out for her. This picture was sent from my uncle, who was living at that time in Israel. And his name is Lipu Egri. He was my father's youngest brother who survived the camp. And this is his second marriage. Her name is Aunt Rose, and they are holding their granddaughter. And this was sent from Israel. I never met him. We were just corresponding with each other. But he passed away since then. Yes. I'm sitting there. This was at my niece's wedding. Next to me, sitting my daughter, Nancy. Behind her is her husband, Danny, who is our favorite son-in-law. Next to him is my husband, Andrew, and next to him is her son, Gerald.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in approximately 1991. This here is myself. I'm holding up my sister's photo, comparing-- this picture is in Washington, DC, in the Holocaust Museum. It's hanging there. It's enlarged. It's very big. And it's right across from the hair, where it's stored, because we are bald here. We were unrecognizable. And when I saw this picture, I really broke down. It's very, very difficult to get all the memories back.
